adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent unauthorised exfiltration of internal source-code repositories through compromise of developer endpoint tooling, including IDE extensions sourced from third-party marketplaces.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Establish and enforce an approved-extension allowlist for all developer IDE environments, restricting installation to verified publisher accounts and version-pinned releases.
Implement continuous monitoring and behavioural detection on developer endpoints to identify anomalous process activity and unauthorised data exfiltration attempts originating from IDE processes.
Enforce least-privilege access controls on internal source-code repositories such that individual developer endpoints cannot bulk-access or clone repositories beyond their defined role scope.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate an enterprise developer fleet using VS Code with unmanaged extension auto-updates, you should immediately deploy an approved-extension allowlist, disable auto-update for unverified publishers, and audit all endpoints for nrwl.angular-console installation within the next 24-72 hours.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and enforce an approved-extension allowlist for all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's Developer Platform Security team must, within 5 business days of May 20 2026, publish and enforce an IDE extension allowlist for all GitHub-issued developer endpoints, restricting VS Code extension installation to a version-pinned, publisher-verified set — explicitly excluding nrwl.angular-console and any extension not signed by a GitHub-approved publisher account. Enforcement must be implemented via GitHub's MDM/endpoint configuration tooling (e.g. Intune or equivalent), and completion is evidenced by a compliance report showing 100% of developer endpoints block unapproved extension installs, with zero exceptions.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control establishes the allowlist requirement generically; this adaptation names the specific malicious extension (nrwl.angular-console) that bypassed controls at GitHub, sets a remediation deadline anchored to the confirmed breach date, and mandates endpoint-enforcement evidence rather than policy-only attestation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and enforce an approved-extension allowlist for all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's VS Code Marketplace trust and safety team must, by May 27 2026, implement an automated re-validation gate that re-scans all currently published extensions against the verified-publisher registry and flags any extension — specifically including publisher account nrwl — where the signing certificate, publisher identity, or binary hash has changed since initial approval, suspending the listing pending manual review. Evidence of completion is a signed audit log confirming the re-validation sweep ran to completion and a report of all extensions suspended or escalated for review.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses allowlisting on the consumer (developer endpoint) side; this adaptation addresses the upstream supply-chain failure — the malicious nrwl.angular-console extension was available on the VS Code Marketplace itself — requiring GitHub to close the publisher-identity verification gap that enabled the initial poisoning.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement continuous monitoring and behavioural detection on developer
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's Security Operations Centre must, within 10 business days of May 20 2026, deploy a behavioural detection rule within its EDR platform targeting VS Code child-process and network-egress patterns on all developer endpoints: specifically, any VS Code extension host process (extensionHost.js) initiating outbound HTTPS connections to non-github.com, non-cdn.vsassets.io destinations while simultaneously reading from local git repository directories must trigger a P1 alert for immediate analyst triage. The rule must be validated against the reconstructed nrwl.angular-console exfiltration telemetry from the May 20 incident, and sign-off by the SOC Lead is required as evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires anomalous-process detection generically; this adaptation encodes the specific exfiltration behaviour observed in the GitHub incident — the VS Code extension host process accessing internal repos and beaconing externally — as a named detection rule, validated against the incident's own telemetry rather than a theoretical baseline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement continuous monitoring and behavioural detection on developer
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's Security Engineering team must, within 15 business days of May 20 2026, implement a DLP control on all developer endpoints that intercepts and alerts on any process reading more than 50 distinct local repository paths (as cloned from github.com/github internal org) within a 60-minute window, with VS Code and its extension host processes explicitly included in the monitored process scope. Alerts must route to the SOC queue with a default quarantine action isolating the endpoint's outbound network access pending analyst review. Completion is evidenced by a test execution report showing the control fires correctly against a simulated bulk-clone scenario, reviewed and signed off by the CISO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses behavioural detection broadly; this adaptation operationalises the specific failure mode — bulk exfiltration of approximately 3,800 repositories from a single employee endpoint — as a rate-based DLP threshold, a gap the generic control does not address.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Enforce least-privilege access controls on internal source-code repositories
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's Internal Platform Access team must, within 10 business days of May 20 2026, audit and remediate the repository access scope of every developer endpoint credential (OAuth token, PAT, or SSH key) associated with the GitHub internal organisation, ensuring no single credential can enumerate or clone more than the repositories explicitly required by the credential owner's current role — using GitHub's own fine-grained PAT scoping and repository-access controls. Any credential found with organisation-wide read scope must be revoked and reissued with role-scoped permissions; completion is evidenced by an access control matrix signed by the Director of Internal Platform Security, showing zero credentials with org-wide repository enumeration rights.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires least-privilege scoping generically; this adaptation anchors it to GitHub's own internal organisation's PAT and OAuth credential model — the specific access mechanism that allowed a single compromised endpoint to exfiltrate 3,800 repositories — and requires remediation of the over-permissioned credentials that made bulk exfiltration possible.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Enforce least-privilege access controls on internal source-code repositories
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's Secret Management team must, within 5 business days of May 20 2026, confirm and document that all secrets with access to the GitHub internal source-code organisation — including the secrets rotated following the May 20 incident — are stored exclusively in GitHub's enterprise secret manager with access scoped to named service identities, and that no developer workstation local environment stores long-lived organisation-level tokens in plaintext git credential helpers or shell profiles. Compliance is evidenced by an automated scan of enrolled developer endpoints producing a report of zero plaintext organisation-scoped tokens, reviewed by the Security Engineering Lead.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses least-privilege access controls on repositories; this adaptation targets the specific post-exfiltration finding that GitHub rotated critical secrets, surfacing the risk that long-lived, broadly scoped credentials persisted on developer workstations — the class of secret that enabled and amplified the bulk-repository access in this incident.

incidents.headline: Microsoft DCU dismantles Fox Tempest malware-signing-as-a-service operation
incidents.summary: On May 19–20, 2026, Microsoft's Digital Crimes Unit (DCU), through an action codenamed OpFauxSign, seized the signspace[.]cloud domain, took hundreds of virtual machines offline, and revoked over 1,000 fraudulently obtained code-signing certificates tied to the Fox Tempest threat actor. Fox Tempest had operated a malware-signing-as-a-service (MSaaS) platform since May 2025, abusing Microsoft's Azure Artifact Signing system to issue 72-hour certificates that allowed ransomware groups including Rhysida, Akira, INC, Qilin, and BlackByte to disguise malware as legitimate software such as AnyDesk, Teams, and PuTTY. Microsoft simultaneously unsealed a civil lawsuit in the US District Court for the Southern District of New York against Fox Tempest and co-conspirator Vanilla Tempest, with the operation linked to attacks across healthcare, education, financial services, and government sectors in the US, France, India, China, and at least six other countries.
incidents.entity: Fox Tempest (cybercrime group — action initiated by Microsoft DCU as disrupting party)

secondary_mappings.why: Microsoft unsealed a federal civil lawsuit in SDNY and obtained a court order to seize Fox Tempest infrastructure, representing formal legal enforcement action.
Microsoft Threat Intelligence tracked the actor since September 2025 and coordinated a multi-partner takedown involving infrastructure seizure and certificate revocation.

Stolen US and Canadian identities were used to fraudulently obtain certificates, and the service's Russian-language communications and prior use against European critical infrastructure indicate possible nation-state adjacency.

incidents.severity_rationale: A cybercrime enabler signing malware for at least five ransomware groups with 1,000+ certificates, thousands of compromised machines globally, and multi-sector victim impact constitutes a high-severity ecosystem disruption event even though the disclosure is a defender action.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: millions of dollars in proceeds (per Microsoft, exact figure not disclosed)

adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent abuse of platform-issued cryptographic credentials to enable malware distribution by establishing controls over identity verification, certificate lifecycle, and anomalous issuance patterns within cloud-based code-signing services.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Establish and maintain a code-signing certificate governance framework including identity verification requirements, issuance rate anomaly detection, and automated revocation workflows.
Implement threat intelligence integration into platform abuse detection pipelines to identify adversarial use of legitimately provisioned cloud services for malware delivery.

Define and execute civil and criminal disruption playbooks for cybercrime-as-a-service operations abusing platform infrastructure, including legal action, domain seizure, and coordinated infrastructure takedown.

internal.name: Microsoft Azure Artifact Signing (Trusted Signing) Service Team
internal.reason: Azure Artifact Signing was the specific Microsoft platform abused by Fox Tempest to issue 1,000+ fraudulent 72-hour code-signing certificates; DCU's remediation directly revoked those certificates and tightened verification controls on this service.
internal.impact_rationale: Platform identity-abuse occurred on this unit's infrastructure; remediation and re-architecture are its direct responsibility.
internal.recommended_action_for_them: Convene an emergency architecture review of Azure Artifact Signing identity-verification pipeline and enforce hardware-bound attestation for all new certificate requests within 72 hours.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a code-signing certificate governance framework
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Azure Artifact Signing service team must implement a hard issuance cap of no more than 10 code-signing certificates per verified organizational identity per 30-day rolling window, with any request exceeding this threshold triggering an automated hold and escalation to the DCU Threat Intelligence team within 4 hours. Identity verification for Azure Artifact Signing must require out-of-band confirmation against Microsoft's existing tenant risk scoring prior to any certificate issuance, closing the gap exploited by Fox Tempest between May 2025 and May 2026 to obtain over 1,000 72-hour certificates under fraudulent organizational identities. Evidence of completion is a deployed policy rule in the Azure Artifact Signing control plane with a test-case audit log showing three synthetic over-threshold requests correctly held and escalated within the 4-hour SLA.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control establishes a governance framework generically; this adaptation directly addresses the specific failure mode — Fox Tempest's exploitation of Azure Artifact Signing's short-lived 72-hour certificate issuance with no apparent volume or identity-integrity gate — and sets concrete numerical thresholds derived from the observed attack scale.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a code-signing certificate governance framework
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Microsoft's certificate authority operations team must retrofit an automated revocation workflow that, upon a DCU-issued bulk-revocation order, can invalidate all certificates sharing a common fraudulent organizational identity root within 2 hours, validated against the May 19–20, 2026 OpFauxSign timeline in which over 1,000 Fox Tempest-linked certificates required simultaneous revocation. The workflow must publish revocation status to Microsoft's Certificate Revocation List endpoints and OCSP responders within 30 minutes of order execution, and the accountable owner (VP, Azure Security Engineering) must sign off a tabletop replay of the OpFauxSign revocation sequence within 60 days of this control's adoption. Completion evidence is a timestamped runbook test report showing end-to-end revocation and CRL/OCSP propagation within the stated SLAs for a synthetic batch of ≥500 certificates.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses revocation workflows generically; this adaptation is anchored to the specific operational bottleneck revealed by the simultaneous need to revoke 1,000-plus certificates across multiple ransomware-affiliated identities in a single coordinated takedown window, requiring a bulk-order capability that a standard per-certificate revocation workflow does not address.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement threat intelligence integration into platform abuse detection
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Microsoft Defender Threat Intelligence (MDTI) team must integrate behavioral signatures specific to the Fox Tempest MSaaS model — namely, Azure Artifact Signing certificate requests followed within 24 hours by binary submissions where the signed payload impersonates AnyDesk, Microsoft Teams, or PuTTY installer metadata — into the Azure platform abuse detection pipeline with a detection rule active no later than 30 days after OpFauxSign closure. The rule must alert the DCU Incident Response queue in real time and automatically suspend the originating Azure tenant pending review, with the accountable owner being the Director of MDTI Engineering. Completion evidence is a deployed detection rule identifier in Microsoft Sentinel, a synthetic red-team test confirming alert generation and tenant suspension within 15 minutes, and a written sign-off from the Director of MDTI Engineering.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control calls for threat intelligence integration into abuse detection pipelines generically; this adaptation encodes the specific Fox Tempest kill-chain pattern — short-lived signing certificates paired with trojanized legitimate-software impersonation — that went undetected for approximately 12 months across the signspace[.]cloud operation and the Azure Artifact Signing system.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement threat intelligence integration into platform abuse detection
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Microsoft's cloud abuse detection team must establish a cross-tenant correlation rule that flags any Azure-hosted virtual machine cluster spun up in excess of 50 VMs within a 72-hour window by a tenant whose code-signing activity involves certificate subjects matching known commercial remote-access or collaboration tool names (AnyDesk, Teams, PuTTY, and their common variant spellings). This rule must be back-tested against the Fox Tempest infrastructure footprint — the hundreds of VMs taken offline during OpFauxSign — to confirm retrospective detection, and must be promoted to production within 45 days. The accountable owner is the Head of Azure Abuse Engineering; completion evidence is a back-test report confirming ≥80% coverage of the known Fox Tempest VM estate and a production deployment ticket with approval timestamp.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses adversarial use of legitimately provisioned cloud services generically; this adaptation targets the specific dual-signal pattern — large-scale VM provisioning co-occurring with impersonation-themed certificate issuance — that characterised Fox Tempest's signspace[.]cloud infrastructure and which represents a distinctive, detectable fingerprint beyond either signal alone.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define and execute civil and criminal disruption playbooks
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Microsoft's DCU Legal and Engineering teams must codify the OpFauxSign execution sequence — domain seizure of signspace[.]cloud, coordinated VM takedown, and parallel civil complaint filing in the US District Court for the Southern District of New York — into a reusable MSaaS disruption playbook with named role accountabilities, pre-authorized legal templates for ex parte domain seizure motions, and a target end-to-end execution time of ≤72 hours from court authorization to full infrastructure takedown. The playbook must explicitly address multi-defendant scenarios (as in OpFauxSign, which named both Fox Tempest and co-conspirator Vanilla Tempest) and include a cross-border notification protocol for affected jurisdictions including France, India, and China. The General Counsel, Microsoft DCU, is accountable; completion evidence is a signed playbook document, a tabletop exercise completion certificate, and a legal pre-authorization memo covering the SDNY ex parte domain seizure mechanism.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control defines disruption playbooks generically; this adaptation locks in the specific legal venue (SDNY), the multi-defendant structure exposed by the Fox Tempest/Vanilla Tempest relationship, the MSaaS-specific infrastructure pattern (domain plus VM fleet plus certificate pipeline), and the international footprint across the named countries, none of which a generic playbook would encode.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define and execute civil and criminal disruption playbooks
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Following OpFauxSign, the DCU Victim Notification team must, within 14 days of any future MSaaS infrastructure takedown, issue sector-specific advisories to healthcare, education, financial services, and government sector CISOs in each affected jurisdiction (US, France, India, China, and the six other countries identified in the Fox Tempest investigation), containing the specific revoked certificate thumbprints, the impersonated software names, and YARA rules for identifying Fox Tempest-signed binaries already present in their environments. The accountable owner is the DCU Director of Victim Outreach; completion evidence is a distribution log showing advisory delivery to at least the sector-designated national CERTs in each named jurisdiction within the 14-day window, with read receipts or acknowledgment tracking.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses coordinated takedown execution but is silent on post-takedown victim remediation; this adaptation captures the downstream obligation revealed by Fox Tempest's broad victimology — spanning five named sectors and ten-plus countries — where downstream organizations holding Fox Tempest-signed malware need actionable indicators to detect and remove it independently of Microsoft's own revocation actions.

incidents.headline: Grafana Labs GitHub environment breached via TanStack npm supply chain attack
incidents.summary: On May 19, 2026, Grafana Labs disclosed that its GitHub environment — including public and private source code repositories and internal operational data — was compromised as a result of the TanStack npm supply chain attack orchestrated by the Coinbase Cartel ransomware group. Grafana stated no customer production systems or the Grafana Cloud platform were affected, and that the breach was limited to business contact names, email addresses, and internal repository content. The incident was part of the broader TeamPCP/Mini Shai-Hulud wave that also hit GitHub and PyPI in the same 48-hour window.

secondary_mappings.why: Breach originated from a compromised upstream npm package (TanStack) used in Grafana Labs' development pipeline.
Private source code, internal operational repositories, and business contact data were accessed and potentially exfiltrated.

incidents.severity_rationale: Confirmed source code and internal data exfiltration from a widely used open-source observability platform, though customer production environments and Grafana Cloud were reported unaffected, limiting immediate customer harm.

sources.title: Grafana GitHub Breach Exposes Source Code via TanStack npm Attack
May 2026 Data Breaches: List Major Incidents & Latest Updates
Cybersecurity News – WIU Cybersecurity Center

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure that software supply-chain dependencies — including third-party npm packages consumed in CI/CD pipelines — are continuously validated for integrity and that compromised workflow tokens are detected and rotated before threat actors can leverage them to exfiltrate source code or internal operational data.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Establish and maintain a software supply-chain security programme that governs the vetting, monitoring, and integrity verification of third-party packages and open-source dependencies used in production and CI/CD environments.

Implement automated secret and token lifecycle management controls, including continuous inventory, scoped least-privilege issuance, and time-bounded rotation of all CI/CD workflow credentials and OIDC tokens.

Define and operationalise an incident response capability that includes ransomware-specific playbooks, covering ransom non-payment governance, law enforcement notification, data-exfiltration triage, and post-incident disclosure obligations.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a software engineering organisation that uses @tanstack/* npm packages or GitHub Actions-based CI/CD pipelines you should immediately audit all GitHub workflow run logs from May 11 2026, search for the Mini Shai-Hulud persistence daemon at ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.user.gh-token-monitor.plist or ~/.config/systemd/user/gh-token-monitor.service, and rotate every cloud and CI secret accessible from any affected runner within 24 hours.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a software supply-chain security programme
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Grafana Labs' Head of Platform Security must, within 5 business days of the May 19 2026 disclosure, produce a complete bill of materials identifying every CI/CD pipeline and GitHub Actions workflow that consumed any version of the compromised TanStack npm packages during the 48-hour TeamPCP/Mini Shai-Hulud wave (May 18–19 2026), cross-referenced against the specific workflow runs that had read or write access to private source code repositories. Evidence of completion is a signed SBOM diff report lodged in the internal security incident record, attesting which pipeline executions were exposed and which were clean.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control governs ongoing supply-chain vetting; this adaptation pins the action to the specific TanStack packages, the exact 48-hour compromise window, and Grafana Labs' GitHub Actions surface — forcing retrospective blast-radius determination rather than a prospective programme posture.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a software supply-chain security programme
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 10 business days, Grafana Labs' Engineering and Security leadership must enforce npm package-lock integrity pinning and Sigstore/npm provenance attestation checks as a mandatory gate on all GitHub Actions workflows that publish to or consume internal npm registries, blocking any package whose provenance chain cannot be verified against the TanStack incident's known compromise indicators. The gate must be demonstrated via a passing CI run on the main branch of at least the three highest-criticality private repositories identified in the May 19 breach scope, with gate configuration committed and peer-reviewed in version control.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires integrity verification generically; this adaptation mandates the specific technical mechanism (Sigstore provenance + lockfile pinning) triggered by the confirmed TanStack attack vector, applied to Grafana Labs' internal GitHub repository estate rather than production deployments.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement automated secret and token lifecycle management controls
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By close of business May 22 2026 (72 hours post-disclosure), Grafana Labs' Platform Security and DevOps leads must revoke and reissue all GitHub Actions OIDC tokens, Personal Access Tokens, and repository-scoped secrets that were active in any workflow referencing TanStack packages during the May 18–19 breach window, replacing them with time-bounded tokens scoped to the minimum repository permissions required for each workflow's declared purpose. Completion evidence is a revocation audit log exported from GitHub's security settings, timestamped entries confirming old credential invalidation, and a new credential inventory entry for each replacement secret in Grafana's secrets management system.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control addresses lifecycle management in the abstract; this adaptation is triggered by the confirmed GitHub environment compromise and sets a named 72-hour deadline tied to the May 19 disclosure date, ensuring credentials potentially observed by the Coinbase Cartel during the breach window cannot be replayed.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement automated secret and token lifecycle management controls
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Grafana Labs' CISO must commission an automated audit, delivered within 15 business days, identifying any CI/CD credential or OIDC token in GitHub Actions workflows that has a scope broader than the specific repository and operation it serves, prioritising workflows that interacted with private source code repositories implicated in the May 19 breach. Each over-scoped credential must be downscoped or replaced with a per-job ephemeral token, with a remediation closure date no later than 30 business days post-disclosure; the final inventory and remediation log must be presented to the CISO as the accountable sign-off authority.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Where the first adaptation addresses immediate revocation, this one targets the structural over-permissioning that amplified blast radius when the Coinbase Cartel accessed Grafana Labs' GitHub environment — a failure mode the incident evidence directly surfaces.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define and operationalise an incident response capability that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Grafana Labs' Legal and Security teams must, within 10 business days of the May 19 2026 disclosure, complete and file the required data-breach notifications to relevant supervisory authorities for each jurisdiction in which the exfiltrated business contact names and email addresses constitute personal data under applicable law (including at minimum GDPR supervisory authorities and any US state regulators triggered by the affected individuals' domiciles), attaching the internal data-exfiltration triage report that establishes the scope of data exposed from private GitHub repositories. The General Counsel is the accountable executive; evidence of completion is dated submission receipts from each notified authority.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control names disclosure obligations generically; this adaptation anchors the obligation to the specific categories of data confirmed exfiltrated (business contacts, email addresses, internal repository content), the Coinbase Cartel as the responsible threat actor, and the multi-jurisdictional regulatory landscape triggered by Grafana Labs' international customer and employee footprint.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define and operationalise an incident response capability that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Grafana Labs' Incident Response lead must, within 20 business days of the May 19 2026 breach disclosure, conduct a formal after-action review of the organisation's response to the TeamPCP/Mini Shai-Hulud wave, specifically evaluating whether the ransomware-specific playbook included detection and containment procedures for npm supply-chain-delivered payloads targeting GitHub Actions, and producing a written gap assessment with prioritised remediation tasks assigned to named owners. The gap assessment must explicitly address the 48-hour detection lag between the wave's initiation and Grafana Labs' disclosure, and the final document must be approved by the CISO and shared with the Board's Risk Committee at its next scheduled meeting.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires ransomware playbooks to exist; this adaptation tests whether those playbooks covered the specific attack vector (npm supply chain via TanStack) and delivery environment (GitHub Actions), using the May 18–19 timeline to define a measurable detection-lag benchmark for the after-action review.

incidents.headline: NYC Health + Hospitals discloses 1.8M-person biometric and medical data breach
incidents.summary: NYC Health + Hospitals Corporation (NYCHHC), the largest public healthcare system in the United States, published a data breach notice on May 19, 2026, confirming that an unauthorized actor accessed its systems via a third-party vendor compromise between approximately November 25, 2025 and February 11, 2026. The attacker exfiltrated highly sensitive data belonging to at least 1.8 million individuals, including medical records, health insurance details, Social Security numbers, government-issued IDs, precise geolocation data, financial account information, and biometric fingerprint and palm-print scans. The incident was reported to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services on March 24, 2026, and NYCHHC is offering 24 months of free credit monitoring through Kroll to affected individuals.

secondary_mappings.why: Breach originated through an unnamed third-party vendor with privileged network access, demonstrating dangerous single-vendor dependency in healthcare IT.
Attackers maintained undetected access for over two months (Nov 25, 2025 to Feb 11, 2026), indicating a material failure in network monitoring and threat detection.
HHS breach notification was filed March 24, 2026; class-action investigators have noted potential failure to notify state attorney general offices as legally required.

incidents.severity_rationale: 1.8 million individuals affected with irreversible biometric data (fingerprints, palm prints), medical records, SSNs, geolocation, and financial credentials exfiltrated; one of the largest US healthcare breaches of 2026 per HHS tracker.

sources.title: NYC Health + Hospitals says hackers stole medical data and fingerprints during breach affecting at least 1.8 million people
Notice of Data Breach
Biometrics, diagnoses, and bank details exposed in major healthcare breach
Millions Impacted Across Several US Healthcare Data Breaches

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure personal and protected health information held across NYCHHC systems and vendor-connected environments is classified, inventoried, and protected commensurate with its sensitivity, including biometric and financial data categories exfiltrated in this incident.

Ensure that third-party vendor access to NYCHHC data environments is governed by enforceable contractual controls, continuous access monitoring, and revocation processes sufficient to detect and terminate unauthorised access within a materially shorter window than the 78-day dwell time observed in this incident.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Establish and maintain a data classification policy that categorises all personal, health, biometric, and financial data by sensitivity and applies commensurate handling, access, and protection requirements.
Implement technical and procedural controls to monitor, log, and alert on data access and exfiltration activity across all environments, including those accessible by third-party vendors.

Establish third-party data access governance including contractual security requirements, business-associate agreements, and periodic access reviews for all vendors with privileged access to sensitive data.
Implement breach detection and response procedures that minimise dwell time and ensure timely notification to affected individuals and relevant regulatory authorities within statutory deadlines.
Apply data minimisation and retention controls so that only data necessary for defined business purposes is collected, retained, and accessible to third parties.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a data classification policy that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: NYC Health + Hospitals Corporation must, within 60 days of this notice (by July 18, 2026), complete a formal reclassification audit of all data categories confirmed exfiltrated in this breach — specifically biometric fingerprint and palm-print scans, precise geolocation data, Social Security numbers, and government-issued IDs — assigning each a Tier 1 (Highest Sensitivity) designation with documented handling requirements that prohibit third-party vendor read access unless explicitly scoped and approved by the CISO. The Chief Information Security Officer is accountable; evidence of completion is a signed data classification register update and a corresponding access control matrix showing enforcement of the new designations across all vendor-accessible environments.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The incident reveals that biometric and geolocation data — categories requiring the most stringent controls — were accessible to a third-party vendor without evidence of a commensurate sensitivity designation; this adaptation forces NYCHHC to close that classification gap for the precise data types compromised, rather than addressing data classification in the abstract.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement technical and procedural controls to monitor
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: NYC Health + Hospitals Corporation must, within 30 days (by June 18, 2026), conduct a forensic gap analysis of its SIEM and DLP tooling to determine why the approximately 79-day exfiltration window (November 25, 2025 to February 11, 2026) produced no automated alert triggering incident response, and must implement — no later than September 30, 2026 — vendor-specific egress monitoring rules that alert within 4 hours on anomalous bulk data transfers involving any of the seven data categories confirmed in this breach. The VP of Information Security is accountable for the gap analysis; the CISO is accountable for rule deployment; evidence of completion is the gap analysis report and documented SIEM rule configurations with test results.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The 79-day dwell time is the defining operational failure of this incident; this adaptation forces NYCHHC to diagnose why its existing monitoring missed a multi-month exfiltration and to implement detection rules calibrated to the specific data types and vendor access paths that were exploited, not just to improve monitoring generically.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish third-party data access governance including contractual security requirements
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: NYC Health + Hospitals Corporation must, within 45 days (by July 3, 2026), identify and inventory every third-party vendor currently holding a business-associate agreement or equivalent contract that grants access to any of the seven data categories exfiltrated in this breach, and must terminate or suspend vendor access for any such vendor where a current, executed BAA with explicit data access scoping, breach notification SLAs of 72 hours or less, and a right-to-audit clause cannot be produced. The Chief Compliance Officer is accountable; evidence of completion is a vendor access inventory signed by the CCO and a remediation log showing contract status for each vendor reviewed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The incident originated via a third-party vendor compromise and NYCHHC's delayed awareness suggests inadequate contractual controls over vendor access and notification obligations; this adaptation mandates an immediate review anchored to the specific contractual requirements — scoped access, 72-hour notification, right to audit — that would have limited either the breach's scope or its detection delay.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement breach detection and response procedures that minimise
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: NYC Health + Hospitals Corporation must conduct a post-incident timeline review, completed by June 30, 2026, that formally documents the decision points between the February 11, 2026 containment date and the March 24, 2026 HHS OCR filing — a 41-day gap — assessing compliance with the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule's 60-day clock and identifying any process failures that delayed individual notification beyond the May 19, 2026 public disclosure. The General Counsel and CISO are jointly accountable; evidence of completion is a written timeline review report reviewed by the Board Audit Committee, with any identified remediation actions assigned owners and deadlines.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The 41-day gap between containment and regulatory filing, followed by a further delay to public disclosure, indicates a breach response workflow that does not enforce statutory notification deadlines as hard operational gates; this adaptation requires NYCHHC to surface and remediate the specific decision-making failures in the February–May 2026 response window rather than simply updating a generic incident response plan.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Apply data minimisation and retention controls so that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: NYC Health + Hospitals Corporation must, within 90 days (by August 18, 2026), complete a data minimisation review specifically for biometric data — fingerprint and palm-print scans — assessing whether the collection and retention of this category is necessary for the operational purposes served by the third-party vendor relationship involved in this breach, and must implement technical controls (including tokenisation or vault isolation) that prevent biometric records from being accessible in any vendor-readable environment unless a documented, time-limited business necessity exists. The Chief Privacy Officer is accountable; evidence of completion is a biometric data inventory, a written necessity assessment signed by the CPO, and a technical architecture diagram showing vault or tokenisation controls.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Biometric data is irreversible once compromised, yet it was stored in an environment reachable by a third-party vendor — this adaptation targets the most irreversible data category in the breach and mandates a necessity and minimisation review specific to NYCHHC's biometric collection practices, which the generic master control does not address at the category or vendor-access level.

incidents.headline: Instructure Canvas LMS breached by ShinyHunters; up to 275M education records stolen
incidents.summary: Instructure, operator of the Canvas learning management system used by thousands of schools and universities worldwide, confirmed in early May 2026 that data was stolen in a cyberattack claimed by the ShinyHunters extortion group. ShinyHunters claims to have exfiltrated records on 275 million users spanning 8,809 educational institutions globally, including student and teacher names, email addresses, usernames, enrolled courses, and private messages. The breach was confirmed by Instructure and widely reported the week of May 13, 2026, making it one of the largest education-sector data breaches ever recorded.

secondary_mappings.why: Attackers gained access to Instructure's platform and extracted user databases at scale, suggesting failures in access controls protecting multi-tenant education data.
Breach affecting 8,800+ institutions and up to 275M records, including minors' data, creates severe trust risk for Instructure across the global education sector.
Exposure of student data at scale across 26+ countries triggers multi-jurisdictional obligations under FERPA, GDPR, UK GDPR, and various national education data laws.

incidents.severity_rationale: Claimed 275M records across 8,800+ institutions represents one of the largest education breaches on record; Instructure confirmed the attack, though the full scale remains under investigation; data includes personal identifiers and private communications.

sources.title: Millions of students' personal data stolen in major education cyberattack
Instructure confirms data breach, ShinyHunters claims attack

adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent unauthorised exfiltration of sensitive personal data held across cloud-hosted platforms through access controls, monitoring, and data minimisation.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Implement data classification and labelling to identify and protect sensitive personal information across all systems.
Enforce data access controls ensuring only authorised users and processes can read, copy, or transmit sensitive data.
Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) tools to detect and block unauthorised exfiltration of personal and sensitive data.
Establish and maintain a data breach response plan including notification procedures for affected individuals and regulators.
Conduct regular third-party penetration testing and vulnerability assessments of data-holding systems and APIs.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement data classification and labelling to identify and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Instructure's Chief Data Officer must, within 30 days of the May 2026 breach confirmation, commission a full classification audit of all data fields stored in Canvas LMS tenant databases across the 8,809 affected institutions, specifically tagging private message content, enrolled course records, and PII fields (name, email, username) as Restricted under Instructure's data classification schema. Each classified data asset must receive a persistent label in the Canvas data catalogue with documented retention justification, and audit completion must be evidenced by a signed classification register submitted to the Board Risk Committee.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control addresses classification broadly; this adaptation targets the precise Canvas data objects — private messages, course enrolment records, and PII — that ShinyHunters exfiltrated, forcing Instructure to establish whether these fields were classified as Restricted prior to the breach and to close any labelling gap that may have contributed to inadequate protective controls around them.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Enforce data access controls ensuring only authorised users
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 14 days of the May 13 2026 breach disclosure, Instructure's VP of Engineering must conduct an emergency review of all API endpoints and service-account permissions in the Canvas LMS production environment that had read or export access to user PII and private message tables, revoking any access not tied to an active, named business justification approved within the last 90 days. Evidence of completion is a time-stamped access control matrix, reviewed and signed by the CISO, showing before-and-after permission states for every service account and API credential in scope.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires authorised access controls generally; this adaptation addresses the specific Canvas API and service-account surface through which 275 million records were bulk-exfiltrated by ShinyHunters, mandating emergency retrospective access right-sizing rather than routine periodic review.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) tools to detect
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Instructure's Security Engineering team must, within 45 days of the May 2026 breach confirmation, deploy DLP rules tuned specifically to Canvas LMS egress paths — including the Canvas Data 2 pipeline, REST and GraphQL API bulk-export endpoints, and any S3-compatible data lake integrations — to alert and block transfers of more than 10,000 user records in a single session without a pre-approved data-sharing agreement on file. Effectiveness must be validated by a red-team simulation of a bulk-export scenario mirroring the ShinyHunters exfiltration vector, with results reported to the CISO within 60 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control mandates DLP deployment without specificity; this adaptation identifies the Canvas-specific egress channels — the Data 2 pipeline and bulk API export paths — that enabled exfiltration of 275 million records, and sets a quantified transaction threshold tied directly to the scale of the ShinyHunters event.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a data breach response plan
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Given that the breach affects students and staff across institutions in multiple jurisdictions (including FERPA-governed US institutions and GDPR-covered EU institutions), Instructure's Legal and Privacy team must, within 7 days of this adaptive control's adoption, produce a jurisdiction-mapped notification matrix identifying the applicable regulatory deadline and supervisory authority for each of the 8,809 affected institutions' home jurisdictions, and must stand up a dedicated breach notification portal allowing affected institutional administrators to confirm impacted user counts by May 31 2026. Evidence of completion is the published notification matrix approved by General Counsel and portal go-live confirmation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a breach response plan; this adaptation forces Instructure to operationalise multi-jurisdictional notification logistics specific to the education-sector regulatory mix — FERPA, GDPR, and state-level breach laws — triggered by a breach of this confirmed scale across 8,809 institutions worldwide.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a data breach response plan
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Instructure's CISO must initiate, within 10 business days of the May 13 2026 public disclosure, a post-incident forensic review specifically scoped to determine the initial access vector, the duration of ShinyHunters' persistence in the Canvas environment, and whether the exfiltration of 275 million records occurred via a single bulk export or incremental transfer; findings must be documented in a Root Cause Analysis report shared with the Board Risk Committee no later than June 30 2026, with each finding mapped to a named remediation owner and target closure date.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control focuses on notification procedures; this adaptation addresses the parallel response obligation to conduct a scoped forensic investigation into the specific ShinyHunters intrusion timeline and exfiltration mechanics, which is prerequisite to accurate regulatory notification and to preventing recurrence through the same vector.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct regular third-party penetration testing and vulnerability assessments
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Instructure must engage an independent penetration testing firm — distinct from its existing security vendors — to conduct a full external and authenticated assessment of the Canvas LMS REST API, GraphQL API, Canvas Data 2 pipeline, and all third-party LTI integration endpoints within 60 days of the May 2026 breach confirmation, with explicit test cases covering bulk data extraction, privilege escalation via service accounts, and unauthenticated or weakly authenticated export paths consistent with the ShinyHunters attack profile. The final report, including all critical and high findings with remediation status, must be reviewed by the Board Audit Committee no later than 90 days post-breach, with a written attestation from the CISO that all critical findings are remediated or have accepted interim mitigating controls.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control mandates periodic penetration testing; this adaptation requires an emergency, scoped assessment targeted at the exact Canvas API and data-pipeline attack surface exploited in the ShinyHunters breach, with explicit test cases mirroring the known exfiltration methodology rather than a routine broad-scope exercise.

incidents.headline: Vodafone source code and infrastructure data publicly leaked by Lapsus$
incidents.summary: In late April 2026, the Lapsus$ extortion group claimed to have breached Vodafone UK's internal network systems and issued a 15-day ransom deadline; on May 12, 2026, after Vodafone declined to engage, Lapsus$ publicly released approximately 7.1 GB of source code and GitHub repository structure data encompassing Vodafone internal projects including OnePortal and Cyberhub. Vodafone confirmed the incident, attributing the breach to compromised third-party development software, and stated that no customer personal data or core network infrastructure was affected. Security researchers warned that hardcoded database credentials embedded in the leaked code could enable follow-on attacks against Vodafone's backend systems.

secondary_mappings.why: Hardcoded credentials found in the leaked source code indicate inadequate secrets management and code security governance practices.
Vodafone attributed the initial compromise to third-party development software, consistent with a software supply chain entry vector.

incidents.severity_rationale: Vodafone confirmed the breach and the 7.1GB leak is publicly accessible; no customer PII confirmed stolen, but hardcoded backend credentials and infrastructure blueprints create material follow-on attack risk for a global telecoms operator.

sources.title: Lapsus$ Exposes Massive Amount of Vodafone Data Online
Vodafone Data Breach Claim Follows LAPSUS$ Data Leak
18th May Threat Intelligence Report
Time expired: Hackers leak Vodafone source code after company refuses to pay

adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent unauthorised exfiltration and public disclosure of proprietary source code and internal infrastructure data held in version-controlled repositories.
Ensure credentials, secrets, and sensitive configuration data embedded in source code or repository artefacts are identified, managed, and rotated to prevent downstream exploitation.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Establish and maintain a data classification policy that categorises source code repositories, infrastructure artefacts, and embedded credentials by sensitivity and access tier.
Implement access control policies restricting repository access to authorised personnel and enforcing least-privilege principles across development toolchains.
Deploy automated secret-scanning controls across all code repositories, CI/CD pipelines, and artefact registries to detect and alert on hardcoded credentials before and after commit.
Maintain a third-party software and tool inventory with associated risk assessments; enforce security baseline requirements for all development tools granted access to source repositories.
Establish a credential rotation programme ensuring all secrets detected in repositories or exfiltrated in a breach are invalidated and replaced within a defined response window.

vendors.name: GitGuardian
vendors.product: GitGuardian Internal Monitoring — enterprise secrets detection and remediation platform covering source code repositories, CI/CD pipelines, container images, and package registries with 500+ secret-type detectors and automated incident workflow.
vendors.coverage_note: Directly addresses MC-DAT-007 and MC-DAT-019; real-time public GitHub firehose monitoring catches secrets exfiltrated to external repos — the precise attack surface exploited in this incident.

vendors.name: Truffle Security (TruffleHog Enterprise)
vendors.product: TruffleHog Enterprise — entropy-based secret scanner with live credential verification across 700+ secret types; validates whether detected credentials are still active, enabling prioritised rotation.
vendors.coverage_note: Addresses MC-DAT-007 and MC-DAT-019; credential verification capability directly prioritises the hardcoded PostgreSQL credentials identified in the Vodafone leaked repos for immediate rotation.

vendors.name: GitHub (Microsoft) — GitHub Advanced Security
vendors.product: GitHub Advanced Security (GHAS) — native secret scanning with push protection, 160+ detectors, and automatic partner-provider notification; integrated directly into GitHub Enterprise repository workflows.
vendors.coverage_note: Addresses MC-DAT-002 and MC-DAT-007; push-protection control would have blocked the hardcoded credential commits at the source had it been enforced on Vodafone's enterprise GitHub organisation.

best_practices.clause: PW.4.1 — Use automated tools to identify hardcoded secrets and credentials in code prior to deployment
best_practices.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF)
best_practices.relevance: Mandates automated pre-commit secret scanning — the control whose absence enabled hardcoded PostgreSQL credential exfiltration.

best_practices.version: Cyber Essentials Plus / NCSC Supply Chain Security Guidance 2021
best_practices.clause: Supply Chain Security — Principle 4: Understand and continually improve your supply chain security posture; validate third-party tool access to sensitive repositories.
best_practices.title: NCSC Supply Chain Security Guidance
best_practices.relevance: Directly addresses the confirmed breach vector: insufficient controls on third-party development software granted access to Vodafone internal repositories.

best_practices.clause: Annex A 8.9 — Configuration management; Annex A 8.25 — Secure development lifecycle; Annex A 5.20 — Addressing information security within supplier agreements
best_practices.title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Information Security Management
best_practices.relevance: Annex A 5.20 and 8.25 jointly require supplier security obligations and secure coding standards that would govern third-party developer tool access and hardcoded credential prevention.

internal.name: Vodafone UK - Group Security Operations & DevOps Engineering
internal.reason: OnePortal and Cyberhub repos directly leaked; hardcoded PostgreSQL credentials expose owned backend systems to immediate follow-on attack.
internal.impact_rationale: Named internal repos leaked with live hardcoded credentials enabling direct backend system compromise.
internal.recommended_action_for_them: Rotate all credentials found in leaked repos immediately and audit all GitHub repositories for additional hardcoded secrets.

historical_analogues.event_name: Samsung source code leak via Lapsus$
historical_analogues.parallel: Lapsus$ exfiltrated and publicly released ~190GB Samsung source code including biometric authentication algorithms after ransom refusal.
historical_analogues.outcome: Samsung confirmed breach; no customer payment data affected but leaked code enabled reverse-engineering of proprietary security logic by third parties.

historical_analogues.event_name: Uber internal systems breach via Lapsus$-linked social engineering
historical_analogues.parallel: Attacker compromised third-party contractor credentials to access Uber's internal GitHub, then found hardcoded AWS and GSuite admin credentials in scripts.
historical_analogues.outcome: Full internal system access achieved; AWS, GCP, HackerOne, and internal dashboards compromised; FTC and DOJ investigations followed.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a UK telecom or enterprise software development environment using GitHub-hosted internal repositories with third-party development tool integrations, you should conduct an emergency full-history secret scan across all repositories, immediately rotate any discovered hardcoded credentials, and audit third-party tool OAuth and token access within the next 24-72 hours.

velocity_signal.brief: Serial telecom repo exfiltration accelerating post-2022 hiatus

incidents.emerging_risk_signal: The Lapsus$-Scattered Spider-ShinyHunters federation emerging in mid-2025 materially expands post-exfiltration exploitation capability, meaning leaked Vodafone credentials face a larger threat-actor pool capable of rapid follow-on attacks.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a data classification policy that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Vodafone's Head of Information Security must, within 10 business days of the May 12 2026 Lapsus$ release, formally reclassify the OnePortal and Cyberhub repositories as Tier-1 Highly Sensitive assets in Vodafone's data classification register, documenting that these repositories contain embedded database credentials and internal infrastructure topology data. The reclassification must trigger an immediate access-tier review and be evidenced by a signed classification record in Vodafone's GRC platform reflecting the updated sensitivity designation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control establishes the classification framework; this adaptation mandates a named post-breach reclassification action for the two specific Vodafone repositories confirmed in the leak, anchored to the 7.1 GB disclosure date and the credential-embedding failure mode that the breach exposed.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement access control policies restricting repository access to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Vodafone's Engineering Governance team must, within 5 business days of May 12 2026, produce a complete access audit of all accounts granted read or write permissions to the OnePortal and Cyberhub GitHub repositories, identifying every third-party developer account and any permissions granted through the compromised third-party development software cited in Vodafone's breach attribution. All non-essential and third-party accounts must be revoked, with residual access explicitly re-authorised in writing by an accountable service owner, and a reconciled permissions matrix submitted to the CISO as evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires least-privilege enforcement broadly; this adaptation directs a time-bounded, repository-specific access audit triggered by the confirmed Lapsus$ breach vector — compromised third-party development tooling — which created an undetected lateral path to source code repositories.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Deploy automated secret-scanning controls across all code repositories
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Vodafone's DevSecOps team must, within 3 business days of May 12 2026, run a retroactive secret-scanning sweep across all commits in the OnePortal and Cyberhub repositories using a tool capable of detecting database connection strings and API keys, producing an itemised report of every hardcoded credential instance with associated commit hash, author, and approximate date of introduction. This report must be delivered to the Incident Response lead as the evidence artefact, and the scanning configuration must be enforced as a blocking pre-commit and PR-merge gate in both repositories before either is reconnected to any CI/CD pipeline.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires deployment of secret-scanning controls; this adaptation responds directly to the security researcher finding that hardcoded database credentials embedded in the leaked 7.1 GB dump could enable follow-on attacks, requiring retroactive coverage of the specific repositories before pipeline access is restored.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Deploy automated secret-scanning controls across all code repositories
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 7 business days of May 12 2026, Vodafone's Incident Response team must cross-reference the secret-scanning report from AC-DAT-007-BCH-01 against Vodafone's live backend database and API gateway inventory to confirm which credential instances found in the leaked OnePortal and Cyberhub code correspond to currently active endpoints. Each matched active credential must be flagged in a tracked remediation register, and the register must be reviewed by the CISO and Infrastructure Lead jointly to prioritise rotation sequencing under AC-DAT-019-BCH-01.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation adds the threat-intelligence step absent from the generic scanning control: it operationalises the specific researcher warning that leaked Vodafone credentials could enable follow-on attacks by requiring active-endpoint matching, translating a detection output into a prioritised remediation queue.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain a third-party software and tool inventory with
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Vodafone's Third-Party Risk team must, within 10 business days of May 12 2026, identify the specific third-party development software named in Vodafone's breach attribution as the initial compromise vector, retrieve or commission an updated security assessment for that tool covering authentication controls, data-access scope, and logging capabilities, and suspend the tool's authorised access to any Vodafone source repository until the assessment confirms it meets Vodafone's security baseline. Evidence of completion is the updated vendor risk record in Vodafone's supplier register with a documented access-reinstatement decision signed by the CISO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a standing inventory and baseline enforcement; this adaptation targets the specific causal failure — that an unnamed third-party development tool was trusted with repository access without sufficient security controls — and requires that tool to be suspended and reassessed before access is restored.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a credential rotation programme ensuring all secrets
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Vodafone's Infrastructure Security team must, using the prioritised remediation register produced under AC-DAT-007-BCH-02, rotate all database credentials and API keys confirmed as both present in the leaked OnePortal and Cyberhub code and active in Vodafone's backend systems, completing high-priority rotations within 24 hours and all remaining rotations within 72 hours of the register being finalised. Each rotation must be logged with the old credential fingerprint, new credential hash, rotation timestamp, and approving engineer, and the completed log must be submitted to the Incident Commander as the closure evidence for this breach's credential-exposure remediation track.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control defines a credential rotation programme generically; this adaptation specifies the two named source repositories, ties the rotation window to the confirmed public leak date of May 12 2026, and enforces a tiered 24/72-hour deadline driven by the active-endpoint risk of follow-on attacks flagged by external researchers.

incidents.headline: Samsung Electronics 50,000-worker strike begins over bonus dispute
incidents.summary: On 21 May 2026, Samsung Electronics' labour union launched an 18-day strike at the company's semiconductor and memory fabs in South Korea after last-minute National Labor Relations Commission mediation collapsed on 20 May when Samsung withheld approval of a proposal the union had accepted. More than 47,000–50,000 union members are participating, disputing the company's 50%-of-salary bonus cap and demanding 15% of operating profit be allocated to performance bonuses, against a backdrop of record Q1 2026 operating profit of ~KRW 57 trillion. South Korean media estimate potential production losses up to KRW 40 trillion (~$27 billion), with the strike described as the largest work stoppage in semiconductor industry history and a direct threat to global AI-chip supply chains.

secondary_mappings.why: 18-day walkout directly threatens fab output; a one-day April preview cut memory fab output 18% and foundry output 58%.
Strike puts Samsung's HBM4 production ramp and AI-chip market-share recovery at risk, threatening its competitive position versus SK Hynix.
Samsung shares fell 3% on strike news; South Korean Finance Minister publicly described the breakdown as 'deeply regrettable', signalling government-level stakeholder pressure.

incidents.severity_rationale: Largest industrial action in semiconductor history involving up to 50,000 workers at the world's dominant memory-chip maker, with estimated potential losses of up to KRW 40 trillion and cascading AI supply-chain exposure.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: Up to KRW 40 trillion (~$27 billion) estimated potential losses per South Korean media; ~$700 million per day production-disruption estimate per Digitimes

Implement workforce engagement and compensation benchmarking programmes to identify and address systemic pay equity gaps before they reach industrial action thresholds.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a structured collective bargaining and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Samsung Electronics' Chief People Officer must convene an emergency board-level labour relations review within 72 hours of strike commencement (by 24 May 2026), specifically to assess whether Samsung's 20 May decision to withhold approval of the National Labor Relations Commission mediation proposal — after the union had already accepted it — constitutes a defined escalation threshold requiring direct board intervention in collective bargaining posture. Evidence of completion: board resolution on record, with a documented decision either to re-engage the NLRC mediation channel or to authorise a revised counter-proposal on the profit-sharing mechanism within five business days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control mandates escalation thresholds and board-level oversight triggers; this adaptation targets the specific failure mode — Samsung's unilateral withdrawal of approval from an already-accepted mediation proposal — as the precise trigger event that should have activated those thresholds, and sets a named accountability chain and deadline to prevent further procedural collapse.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a structured collective bargaining and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Samsung Electronics' Head of Industrial Relations, in coordination with external Korean labour law counsel, must document and publish internally a structured mediation re-engagement protocol specific to the current NLRC process, defining: the maximum permissible interval between union acceptance and management ratification of any mediator proposal (not to exceed 48 hours), the designated approval authority (no lower than Executive Vice President level), and the conditions under which Samsung may withhold ratification. This protocol must be submitted to the NLRC as a good-faith procedural commitment no later than 27 May 2026, with the written submission serving as the completion artefact.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires defined mediation protocols; this adaptation directly addresses the specific procedural failure on 20 May 2026 where the absence of a ratification timeline and authority boundary allowed Samsung to block an accepted proposal, and imposes concrete parameters to prevent recurrence within the active 18-day strike window.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and maintain a structured collective bargaining and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Samsung Electronics' VP of Supply Chain Risk, in conjunction with the semiconductor fab operations lead at Hwaseong and Pyeongtaek, must activate a daily production-loss reporting cadence for the duration of the strike (21 May – 7 June 2026), quantifying actual output loss against the South Korean media estimate of up to KRW 40 trillion, and linking each day's cumulative figure to a pre-defined settlement-urgency threshold — specifically, that if cumulative losses exceed KRW 10 trillion, an automatic brief to the Samsung Electronics Co-CEO is triggered within 24 hours with a mandate to present a revised bonus proposal to the union. Evidence of completion: daily loss report log maintained by the VP of Supply Chain Risk, with board brief records on file for any threshold-breach day.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires escalation thresholds tied to board-level oversight; this adaptation creates a financial loss-linked escalation mechanism anchored to the specific KRW-scale production risk at the named Hwaseong and Pyeongtaek fabs, transforming an abstract oversight trigger into a quantified, time-bound decision rule active within the current strike window.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement workforce engagement and compensation benchmarking programmes to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Samsung Electronics' Chief Financial Officer and Chief People Officer must jointly commission, within 30 days of strike resolution, an independent compensation benchmarking study comparing Samsung's 50%-of-salary bonus cap against: (a) disclosed bonus-to-profit allocation ratios at TSMC, SK Hynix, and Micron for fiscal years 2024 and 2025, and (b) South Korean large-cap manufacturing sector norms under the Korean Fair Trade Commission's annual disclosure data. The study must assess whether a 15%-of-operating-profit allocation — as demanded by the union against a Q1 2026 operating profit base of approximately KRW 57 trillion — is within or outside peer-market norms, with findings presented to the Samsung Compensation Committee no later than 60 days post-resolution. The commissioned study report, with the Compensation Committee's written response, constitutes the completion artefact.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires benchmarking to identify systemic pay equity gaps before they reach industrial action thresholds; this adaptation targets the specific compensation structure dispute — Samsung's fixed bonus cap versus a profit-linked demand — and grounds the benchmarking exercise in named peer companies and the concrete financial parameters of the 2026 dispute, replacing a general surveillance obligation with a time-bound, post-incident corrective study.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement workforce engagement and compensation benchmarking programmes to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Samsung Electronics' Head of Workforce Engagement must establish, within 90 days of the strike's end, a standing joint labour-management Profit Sharing Review Panel — composed of four union representatives from the Samsung Electronics Labour Union and four management nominees below Executive Vice President level — to convene semi-annually and review the relationship between declared operating profit and the bonus allocation formula, with the explicit mandate to flag any quarter in which operating profit growth exceeds 20% year-on-year while the bonus cap remains static, and to escalate that flag to the Board Compensation Committee within 30 days. Panel charter, first meeting minutes, and Compensation Committee acknowledgement letter constitute the completion artefacts.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires proactive workforce engagement programmes to detect systemic pay gaps before industrial action; this adaptation responds to the specific structural gap exposed in 2026 — a static bonus cap persisting alongside record KRW 57 trillion quarterly profit — by institutionalising a named, recurring review mechanism with a quantified profit-growth trigger rather than relying on ad hoc engagement.

incidents.headline: Meta Platforms begins 8,000-job layoffs, largest round since 2023
incidents.summary: On 20 May 2026, Meta Platforms commenced layoff notifications to approximately 8,000 employees — 10% of its global workforce of 78,865 — in three waves beginning at 04:00 local time, with a further 6,000 open positions cancelled, bringing the total headcount reduction to 14,000. The cuts span Reality Labs, the Facebook product organisation, recruiting, sales, and global operations, and arrive in the same week Meta reported record quarterly revenue of $56.31 billion and net income of $26.8 billion. California WARN Act filings confirmed cuts at the Burlingame and Sunnyvale campuses, and Chief People Officer Janelle Gale cited AI-driven efficiency and offsetting capital investment as rationale; further rounds are expected in August and autumn 2026.

secondary_mappings.why: Layoffs fund a reallocation of up to $145 billion in 2026 AI capital expenditure and a structural shift to AI-focused 'pod' teams under the Superintelligence Labs division.

Internal employee rating on Blind fell 25% from its 2024 peak with a 39% culture-score drop, and internal petitions have gathered over 1,000 signatures, signalling significant workforce trust erosion.

incidents.severity_rationale: 8,000 confirmed layoffs (14,000 effective with cancelled roles) at one of the world's largest technology companies on a single execution date, with additional waves planned, generating significant workforce and reputational impact.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: Severance costs to be recognised in Q2 2026; $1 billion restructuring charge equivalent not explicitly disclosed but AI capex guidance raised to $125–145 billion

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure the organisation maintains a documented, fair, and legally compliant process for workforce reduction decisions, including selection criteria, affected population mapping, and statutory notification obligations.
Ensure the organisation protects the continuity of critical operational capabilities and institutional knowledge during and after large-scale workforce restructuring events.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Workforce change events must be governed by a documented decision framework that includes legal review, selection-criteria validation, and board or senior leadership sign-off before notification commences.

All statutory notification requirements — including WARN Act, GDPR Article 88, and equivalent jurisdiction-specific obligations — must be identified, tracked, and fulfilled prior to or concurrent with employee notification.
Organisations must maintain a critical-role registry that is reviewed and updated before any workforce reduction event to prevent unintended elimination of single points of failure.

Severance, outplacement, and transition support programmes must be documented and communicated to affected employees at time of notification in accordance with applicable law and organisational policy.

Where AI or algorithmic tools are used in workforce selection, retention scoring, or role categorisation, the organisation must conduct a bias and disparate-impact review prior to executing decisions at scale.

incidents.headline: Standard Chartered announces 7,800 back-office job cuts by 2030
incidents.summary: On 19 May 2026, Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters announced at an investor day in Hong Kong that the bank would eliminate more than 15% of its corporate-functions workforce — approximately 7,800 positions — by 2030, explicitly framing the cuts as replacing 'lower-value human capital' with AI-driven automation. The reduction targets HR, risk, compliance, and support-services roles across the bank's ~80,000-person global workforce, and was disclosed alongside record Q1 2026 operating income of $5.9 billion and a return-on-tangible-equity target of 18% by 2030. Standard Chartered's Hong Kong-listed shares rose 2.5% on the announcement, underscoring investor appetite for AI-led headcount reduction.

secondary_mappings.why: Cuts are embedded in a formal 2030 growth plan targeting 18% return on tangible equity through AI-led operating model transformation.
CEO's 'lower-value human capital' framing generated immediate public backlash and created reputational exposure for the bank's employer brand across Asia, Africa, and the Middle East.
Cuts target compliance and risk functions, raising questions for regulators in multiple jurisdictions about sustained supervisory-function staffing levels.

incidents.severity_rationale: Nearly 7,800 roles eliminated over a multi-year horizon with immediate disclosure impact; severity moderated relative to the Samsung strike by the phased timeline to 2030 and the absence of immediate operational disruption.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: Record Q1 2026 operating income $5.9 billion disclosed; direct restructuring cost not separately quantified in public statements
